questions.question: You and another car arrive at a 4-way stop at the exact same moment. The other car is on your right and you're both going straight. Who goes first?
choices: You do — left has priority at 4-way stops
The other driver — when arrivals tie, yield to the car on your right
Whoever is going straight has priority over turners
questions.explanation: When two vehicles arrive at a 4-way stop simultaneously, the vehicle on the right has the right-of-way. Handbook p. 60.

questions.question: At a 4-way stop, who goes first if you arrive a moment before any other vehicle?
choices: The car on your right, always
questions.explanation: First to arrive at a 4-way stop goes first. The yield-to-right rule only resolves tied arrivals. Handbook p. 60.

questions.question: You arrive at an intersection. Another driver is already partway through it. What do you do?
choices: Honk to warn them you're proceeding
Speed up because you have the right-of-way at the intersection
questions.explanation: Yield to any driver already in the intersection before you. Handbook p. 60.

questions.question: You're stopped at a red light. An ambulance with lights and siren comes up behind you. What do you do?
choices: Run the red light immediately to clear the way
Stay put — the light is red, the ambulance will go around
Pull to the right when safe and stop; clear the intersection cautiously only if you can do so safely
Honk and wait for the light to turn green
questions.explanation: Yield to emergency vehicles by pulling to the right and stopping. If you're at an intersection, clear it carefully — don't run a red light unsafely. Handbook p. 86.

questions.question: When an emergency vehicle approaches with lights and siren on, what must you do?
choices: Speed up to get out of their way
Drive immediately to the right side of the road and stop until it passes
Continue at normal speed but signal
questions.explanation: Yield to emergency vehicles: drive to the right edge of the road and stop until they pass. Handbook p. 86.

questions.question: You're driving south on a 4-lane divided highway with a grass median. A school bus heading north on the other side of the median stops with red lights flashing. What do you do?
choices: Stop — all directions must stop for a school bus with flashing reds
You're not required to stop — the median separates traffic, so only northbound (behind the bus) must stop
Slow to 20 mph and proceed with caution
Pull to the right shoulder and stop
questions.explanation: On a divided highway with a median (4+ lanes), only vehicles behind the bus must stop. Opposite-direction traffic across a median is not legally required to stop. Handbook p. 46.

questions.question: You're driving on a 2-lane road. A school bus traveling the opposite way stops with its red lights flashing. What must you do?
choices: Continue at posted speed — opposite traffic isn't affected
Stop immediately before reaching the bus, both directions must stop on a 2-lane road
Slow to 20 mph and pass with caution
Honk to warn kids you're coming
questions.explanation: On a 2-lane road, traffic in both directions must stop for a school bus with flashing red lights. Handbook p. 46.

questions.question: You're driving on a 4-lane road with no median, just painted lines between directions. A school bus heading the opposite way stops with flashing red lights. What do you do?
choices: Continue — 4 lanes always means opposite traffic continues
Stop — without a physical median, both directions must stop
Only stop if you see kids
questions.explanation: On a 4-lane road WITHOUT a median, both directions must stop. The median is the deciding factor, not the lane count. Handbook p. 46.

questions.question: You're driving on a 5-lane road with a shared center turn lane. A school bus on the opposite side stops with flashing red lights. Must you stop?
choices: No, opposite-direction traffic is not legally required to stop on this road type
Yes, but only if you're within 100 feet
questions.explanation: On a 5+ lane road with a shared center turn lane, only vehicles behind the bus must stop. Opposite direction is not legally required. Handbook p. 46.

questions.question: A school bus directly ahead of you turns on its alternating flashing red lights. What must you do?
choices: Slow down to 20 mph and pass carefully
Stop immediately before reaching the bus and don't proceed until the flashing red lights stop
Honk to warn kids and continue at the posted limit
Pass only if there's a clear path
questions.explanation: Stop immediately before reaching a school bus with flashing red lights. Don't proceed until the lights stop flashing. Handbook p. 46.

questions.question: What's the maximum fine for illegally passing a school bus with its red lights flashing?
questions.explanation: Fines for illegally passing a school bus can run up to $3,000 — plus insurance rate increases. Handbook p. 46.

questions.question: If you illegally pass a school bus with flashing red lights, what's a likely consequence besides the fine?
choices: Your insurance rates will likely increase
Loss of driving privileges for 5 years
Nothing — the fine is the only consequence
questions.explanation: A conviction for passing a school bus illegally usually means an insurance rate increase. Handbook p. 46.

questions.question: On a 2-lane road, you reach a 4-way stop. Past the intersection, a school bus stops with red lights flashing to unload kids. Another car arrives at the 4-way stop on your right at the same time. What do you do?
choices: Yield to the right then proceed past the bus
Proceed straight through — you got there first
Wait — the school bus rule requires you to stop and not proceed past it until the red lights stop
Drive around the bus on the right
questions.explanation: On a 2-lane road, both directions must stop for a school bus with flashing red lights. The 4-way stop rule is moot — nobody can proceed past the bus until the lights stop. Handbook p. 46.

questions.question: You stop at a railroad crossing — gates down, lights flashing, train approaching. An ambulance with lights and siren pulls up behind you. What do you do?
choices: Drive around the gates to clear the way for the ambulance
Pull onto the tracks to make room
Wait — trains have the right-of-way 100% of the time, even over emergency vehicles. The ambulance must wait too
Reverse onto the shoulder past the ambulance
questions.explanation: Trains have the right-of-way over ambulances, fire engines, cars, police, and pedestrians. Never drive around lowered gates. Handbook p. 96.

questions.question: True or false: 'Trains have the right-of-way 100% of the time — over ambulances, fire engines, cars, police, and pedestrians.'
choices: False — emergency vehicles always have priority
False — only freight trains have this priority
True only at protected crossings with gates
questions.explanation: Trains have right-of-way 100% of the time — over ambulances, fire engines, cars, police, and pedestrians. Handbook p. 96.

questions.question: You approach a roundabout. A pedestrian is in the crosswalk at your entry, and a car is circulating in the roundabout heading toward your entry. What do you do?
choices: Enter — circulating cars yield to entering cars
Yield to the pedestrian only, then enter
Yield to BOTH the pedestrian and the car in the circle. Enter only when clear of both
Stop completely as if at a stop sign
questions.explanation: At a roundabout: yield to pedestrians AND to traffic already in the circle before entering. Handbook p. 55.

questions.question: Which direction does traffic flow in a roundabout?
choices: Either direction is OK if signaled
Depends on the size of the roundabout
questions.explanation: Roundabouts flow counterclockwise. Handbook p. 13.

questions.question: You're inside a roundabout approaching the exit you want to take. What should you do?
choices: Brake hard so cars behind know you're exiting
Wave your hand out the window
questions.explanation: Inside a roundabout, signal your intention to exit so other drivers know your plan. Handbook p. 55.

questions.question: As you approach a roundabout, what's the first thing you should do?
choices: Stop completely at the entry line
questions.explanation: Slow down as you approach a roundabout, then yield to pedestrians and traffic in the circle. Handbook p. 55.

questions.question: Power outage. You arrive at an intersection with traffic signals that are completely dark. Another car arrives on your right at the same time. What do you do?
choices: Treat as a 4-way stop tie — yield to the car on the right after both stop
Slow down and proceed if no one is coming
Whoever is going straight has priority
questions.explanation: If signals are out, come to a complete stop first, then yield to the vehicle on the right. Handbook p. 56.

questions.question: Traffic lights at an intersection aren't working. What's the rule?
choices: Treat as a green light — proceed if clear
Come to a complete stop, then yield to any vehicle on your right
First to arrive goes — no need to stop fully if you're alone
questions.explanation: Signal out = complete stop + yield to the right. Handbook p. 56.

questions.question: You have a solid GREEN light and want to turn left. Oncoming cars are going straight through. What do you do?
choices: Go — green means you have priority to turn
Yield to oncoming traffic — a solid green doesn't give you priority for a left turn
Inch out so oncoming traffic yields to you
questions.explanation: On a solid green, left-turners must yield to oncoming traffic. A green ARROW would give priority — a solid green does not. Handbook p. 60.

questions.question: When making a left turn at a signalized intersection on a solid green light, who has the right-of-way?
choices: You do — the left-turner has priority once signaled
questions.explanation: Left-turners yield to oncoming traffic on a solid green. Handbook p. 60.

questions.question: You're pulling out of your driveway onto a residential street. A car is coming down the street. Who has the right-of-way?
choices: You — first to be there has priority
The car on the street — driveway traffic yields to street traffic
You — but only if you signal first
questions.explanation: When entering a street from a driveway or private road, yield to traffic already on the street. Handbook p. 60.

questions.question: Before pulling out of an alley, driveway, or private road onto a street, you must:
choices: Slow down and proceed if clear
Yield only if signs are posted
questions.explanation: Complete stops are required when entering a street from an alley, driveway, private road, or building. Handbook p. 45.

questions.question: A pedestrian carrying a white cane begins to cross a side street as you approach. There's no marked crosswalk. What must you do?
choices: Honk to alert them you're coming
Come to a complete stop — the white cane requires a full stop regardless of crosswalk markings
Only yield if they're in a marked crosswalk
questions.explanation: A pedestrian with a white cane (or guide dog) requires a full STOP, marked crosswalk or not. Handbook p. 91.

questions.question: What do you do when you see a pedestrian using a guide dog or white cane?
choices: Honk so they know you're there
Pass them on the left at normal speed
questions.explanation: White cane or guide dog = full stop. Handbook p. 91.

questions.question: You approach a work zone intersection. The traffic light shows GREEN in your direction, but a flagger holds a STOP paddle toward you. What do you do?
choices: Obey the light — it's the highest authority
Obey the flagger — flaggers override signs and signals
questions.explanation: A flagger's directions override traffic signs and signals in a work zone. Handbook p. 59.

questions.question: A police officer at an intersection is directing traffic. The light is green in your direction, but the officer signals you to STOP. What do you do?
choices: Stop — officer directions override traffic signals
Wave at the officer to clarify
questions.explanation: Officer directions override traffic signals. Handbook p. 56.

questions.question: You're driving downhill on a narrow one-lane mountain road. Another car is coming uphill. There's no room to pass. Who backs up?
choices: The uphill car — they have less momentum
You do — downhill yields to uphill on narrow mountain roads
questions.explanation: On a narrow mountain road, downhill drivers yield to uphill drivers. The uphill driver has more difficulty restarting. Handbook p. 69.

questions.question: You're on the on-ramp to I-15 and traffic on the interstate is heavy with no clear gap. What do you do?
choices: Push in regardless — interstate must yield to merging traffic
Use the acceleration lane to match speed, then merge at a gap. Yield — interstate traffic has priority
Stop on the ramp and wait for a gap
Drive on the shoulder past the slow traffic
questions.explanation: On-ramp traffic yields to interstate traffic. Use the acceleration lane to match speed, then merge when safe. Handbook p. 60.

questions.question: Who has the right-of-way when traffic on an entrance ramp meets traffic on the interstate?
choices: The ramp traffic — they're getting up to speed
questions.explanation: Drivers already on the interstate have the right-of-way. Ramp drivers must yield. Handbook p. 60.

questions.question: A TRAX light rail train approaches a grade crossing in downtown Salt Lake City — lights flashing, gates lowering. Can you try to beat it?
choices: Yes — TRAX is slower than freight trains
Yes if it's still a block away
No — TRAX can reach 65 mph in 21 seconds. Stop and wait like any railroad crossing
Yes if the gate isn't all the way down
questions.explanation: TRAX is treated like any railroad crossing. TRAX trains reach 65 mph in 21 seconds — don't try to beat them. Handbook p. 97.

questions.question: About how much distance does a 100-car freight train at 55 mph need to stop in an emergency?
choices: More than a mile (~18 football fields)
questions.explanation: A freight train at 55 mph needs more than a mile to stop. Don't assume a train can stop for you. Handbook p. 97.

questions.question: On a 4-lane road, a regular passenger car is stopped on the right shoulder with hazard lights flashing. It's NOT an emergency vehicle. What must you do?
choices: Maintain speed — the Move Over law only applies to emergency vehicles
Slow down and change lanes away from the stopped vehicle if practical
Stop completely on the shoulder behind them
Honk to ask if they need help
questions.explanation: The Move Over Law applies to ANY stationary vehicle with hazards — not just emergency vehicles. Slow and change lanes if practical. Handbook p. 86.

questions.question: The Move Over Law requires you to slow and change lanes for which vehicles?
choices: Any stationary vehicle with flashing hazard lights
questions.explanation: Move Over applies to ALL stationary vehicles with flashing hazards. Handbook p. 86.

questions.question: You see a stationary vehicle with amber lights flashing in or near an HOV lane. What does the Move Over Law require?
choices: Change lanes OUT of the HOV lane if practical
Speed up to clear the area faster
questions.explanation: For HOV-adjacent stationary amber lights, change lanes OUT of the HOV lane if practical. Handbook p. 86.

questions.question: What's the penalty for failing to attend the required course after a Move Over Law violation?
questions.explanation: Move Over violations require attending a 4-hour defensive driving course within 90 days. Failure = 90-day suspension. Handbook p. 86.

questions.question: At a left turn, your signal is a FLASHING YELLOW ARROW. Oncoming traffic is heavy and a pedestrian is starting to cross the perpendicular crosswalk in your turning path. What do you do?
choices: Turn — flashing yellow arrow means you have priority
Yield to both oncoming traffic and the pedestrian. Turn only when both are clear
Honk so the pedestrian backs up
questions.explanation: A flashing yellow arrow allows turns but you must yield to oncoming traffic AND pedestrians. Handbook p. 56.

questions.question: What does a flashing yellow arrow mean?
choices: Turn allowed — but yield to oncoming traffic and pedestrians
Same as a green arrow — you have priority
Stop and wait for a steady arrow
questions.explanation: Flashing yellow arrow = turn permitted, but yield to oncoming traffic and pedestrians. Handbook p. 56.

questions.question: At 7:50 a.m. you drive past an elementary school. The school zone speed sign lights are flashing but you don't see any kids. What's your speed?
choices: The posted regular limit — no kids visible
The school zone speed (20 mph) — the flashing lights mean the lower limit is active
questions.explanation: Flashing school-zone lights = the slower school zone speed applies, kids visible or not. Handbook p. 91.

questions.question: What's the maximum speed when passing a school during recess or when school-zone lights are flashing?
questions.explanation: 20 mph in a school zone when children are present, lights are flashing, or during school opening/closing hours. Handbook p. 54.

questions.question: You're stopped at a railroad crossing — gates down, train passing through. A police car with lights and siren pulls up behind you. Across the tracks, an ambulance approaches from the other side. What's the right outcome?
choices: The police car overrides the train signal — you proceed
Drive around the gate to clear the way for the police car
All three of you wait. Trains have right-of-way over ambulances, fire engines, cars, AND police
Reverse off the road so the police car can pass
questions.explanation: Trains beat everything — including police and ambulances. Wait until gates raise and lights stop flashing. Handbook p. 96.

questions.question: Which statement best matches the handbook's rule about right-of-way?
choices: Right-of-way is yours when you arrive first
Right-of-way is something you give, not take — never assume you have it
Right-of-way always belongs to the larger vehicle
questions.explanation: Never assume you have the right-of-way — it's something you GIVE, not take. Handbook p. 60.

questions.question: You have a green light. A pedestrian is still finishing crossing the intersection. What do you do?
choices: Yield to the pedestrian still in the intersection
Edge forward to claim your turn
questions.explanation: Yield to pedestrians, bicyclists, and others still in the intersection — even when you have a green. Handbook p. 60.

questions.question: You have a GREEN light at an intersection. A pedestrian is in the crosswalk crossing in front of you. What do you do?
choices: Proceed — green gives you priority over pedestrians
Yield to the pedestrian even on a green
Tap the horn to alert them
questions.explanation: Even with a green light, yield to pedestrians in the crosswalk. Handbook p. 91.

questions.question: Pedestrians have the right-of-way at:
questions.explanation: Yield to pedestrians in marked OR unmarked crosswalks. Handbook p. 54, p. 91.

questions.question: A car ahead of you is stopped at a crosswalk. What's the rule?
choices: Pass on the right if there's room
Pass on the left if no oncoming traffic
Never pass a vehicle stopped at a crosswalk — pedestrians you can't see may be crossing
Pass only if you honk first
questions.explanation: Never pass a vehicle stopped at a crosswalk — they may be yielding to a pedestrian you can't see. Handbook p. 92.

questions.question: If you have to walk along a road with no sidewalk, which side should you walk on?
choices: Right shoulder, same direction as traffic
questions.explanation: Walk on the LEFT shoulder facing traffic so you can see vehicles approaching. Handbook p. 91.

questions.question: When stopping at a crosswalk where a pedestrian is crossing, where should you stop?
choices: On top of the crosswalk to claim priority
Far enough back that you can clearly see the pedestrians and they can see you
Right at the edge of the crosswalk only
Wherever, as long as you stop
questions.explanation: Stop far enough back from the crosswalk to maintain visibility of pedestrians. Handbook p. 91.

questions.question: A solid GREEN light means:
choices: Go through with caution if the way is clear
Slow down and prepare to stop
questions.explanation: Green = proceed with caution IF the way is clear. Handbook p. 55.

questions.question: What does a green ARROW mean?
choices: You may proceed in the arrow's direction after yielding to anyone still in the intersection
Same as a flashing yellow — yield to all traffic
Stop and wait for a steady green
You have absolute priority — others must stop
questions.explanation: Green arrow = proceed in the arrow's direction after yielding to vehicles and pedestrians still in the intersection. Handbook p. 55.

questions.question: A steady YELLOW light means:
choices: Speed up to beat the red
Stop if you can do so safely — the light is about to turn red
Yield to traffic on the cross street
Caution only — proceed at normal speed
questions.explanation: Yellow = light is about to change to red. Stop if you can safely. Handbook p. 55.

questions.question: A flashing YELLOW light means:
choices: Stop completely as if at a stop sign
Slow down and proceed with caution; be prepared to stop
questions.explanation: Flashing yellow = slow down, proceed with caution, be prepared to stop. Handbook p. 55.

questions.question: A steady RED light requires you to:
choices: Slow down and proceed if clear
Yield to cross traffic, then go
Come to a complete stop before the intersection and wait until green
Stop only if traffic is coming
questions.explanation: Red = complete stop before the intersection. Wait until green. Handbook p. 56.

questions.question: At a red light, you want to turn right. What's the rule?
choices: Allowed after a complete stop, unless a sign prohibits it
Allowed without stopping if no traffic is coming
questions.explanation: Right on red is allowed after a complete stop, unless posted otherwise. Handbook p. 56.

questions.question: A flashing RED light means:
choices: Treat it like a stop sign — full stop, look both ways, proceed when safe
Same as a steady red — wait for green
questions.explanation: Flashing red = stop and look both ways, then proceed when safe. Same as a stop sign. Handbook p. 56.

questions.question: A red ARROW (left or right) means:
choices: Same as a flashing yellow arrow — yield
Proceed only if no traffic is coming
questions.explanation: Red arrow = turn prohibited. Stay stopped until indicated to proceed. Handbook p. 56.

questions.question: If a red left arrow won't change and traffic conditions allow, what exception applies?
choices: You may turn after stopping if going from a one-way to a one-way street and it's safe
You may always turn after waiting 2 minutes
questions.explanation: If stranded by a non-changing red left arrow, you may turn from one-way to one-way if safe. Handbook p. 56.

questions.question: At a STOP sign, what kind of stop is required?
choices: A rolling stop if no traffic is visible
questions.explanation: Stop signs require a COMPLETE stop. Handbook p. 45.

questions.question: Three cars arrive at a 4-way stop simultaneously: one across from you, one to your right, one to your left. Who goes first?
choices: You — you have the most cars to deal with
questions.explanation: Tied arrivals at a 4-way stop = yield to the right. Handbook p. 60.

questions.question: On a 2-lane road, which direction(s) of traffic must stop when a school bus shows flashing red lights?
questions.explanation: On a 2-lane road, both directions stop. Handbook p. 46.

questions.question: On a divided highway (4+ lanes with a median), which traffic must stop for a school bus with flashing red lights?
choices: Only vehicles behind the bus (same direction)
Only the lane right next to the bus
questions.explanation: Median present = only same-direction (behind the bus) must stop. Handbook p. 46.

questions.question: True or false: on a 4-lane road WITHOUT a median, only same-direction traffic must stop for a school bus.
choices: False — both directions must stop on a 4-lane road without a median
True if it's 35 mph or under
True if the bus is in the center lane
questions.explanation: No median = both directions stop. Handbook p. 46.

questions.question: When you're stopped for a school bus with flashing red lights, when may you proceed?
choices: When you can no longer see kids near the bus
When the flashing red lights stop
When you hear the bus engine start
questions.explanation: Don't proceed until the flashing red lights stop. Handbook p. 46.

questions.question: At a railroad crossing controlled by gates or signals, how far from the nearest rail should you stop?
choices: At least 5 feet but no more than 25 feet
More than 15 feet but not more than 50 feet
questions.explanation: Stop more than 15 feet but not more than 50 feet from the nearest rail. Handbook p. 95.

questions.question: Flashing lights at a railroad crossing mean:
choices: Slow down and proceed if no train is visible
You MUST stop — do not proceed while lights flash
Stop only if gates are down
questions.explanation: Flashing RR lights = mandatory stop. Don't proceed while flashing. Handbook p. 95.

questions.question: If a railroad crossing gate is down, may you drive around it?
choices: Yes, if you can see no train is coming
Yes, if you're in a hurry
Never — it's illegal regardless of visibility
Yes, if you're an emergency vehicle
questions.explanation: Never drive around or under a lowered RR gate. Handbook p. 95.

questions.question: What does an X-shaped white sign reading 'Railroad Crossing' (crossbuck) mean?
choices: Stop if a train is visible; yield to trains
questions.explanation: A crossbuck means yield to trains; stop if a train approaches. Handbook p. 96.

questions.question: A round YELLOW sign with a black 'X' and 'RR' lettering means:
choices: Railroad crossing ahead — slow, look, listen
questions.explanation: Round yellow RR advance-warning sign tells you a railroad crossing is ahead. Slow, look, listen. Handbook p. 57.

questions.question: You're approaching railroad tracks. Traffic on the far side has stopped and is backing up. What should you do?
choices: Roll onto the tracks slowly to keep up with traffic
Stop before the tracks — never stop on them. Wait until you can clear them completely
Honk for the cars ahead to move
Drive over the tracks faster to clear them
questions.explanation: Don't stop on tracks. If traffic could trap you, wait at the stop line before crossing. Handbook p. 57.

questions.question: True or false: it's never legal to pass another vehicle at a railroad crossing.
choices: False — passing is OK if the gates are up
True only if the lights are flashing
False — passing is OK at uncontrolled crossings
questions.explanation: Never pass another vehicle at a railroad crossing. Handbook p. 44.

questions.question: If the lights at a railroad crossing appear broken, what's required before you cross?
choices: You may proceed at normal speed
Stop, then proceed only if you have at least 1 mile of clear sight in both directions and no train
Cross only between 9 a.m. and 5 p.m.
questions.explanation: If RR lights appear broken: full stop, then proceed only if 1+ mile of clear sight both directions and no train. Handbook p. 95.

questions.question: Your car stalls on railroad tracks and you can see a train coming. What do you do?
choices: Try to restart and drive off
Get out and run AWAY from the tracks at an angle toward the approaching train
Stay in the car and brace for impact
Push the car off the tracks
questions.explanation: If stalled on tracks with a train coming: exit immediately and run at an angle TOWARD the train (so flying debris from impact goes behind you). Handbook p. 97.

questions.question: How close may you follow a fire vehicle responding to an alarm?
questions.explanation: Don't follow within 500 feet of a fire vehicle answering an alarm. Handbook p. 86.

questions.question: You see a fire truck responding to an alarm on a city block. What's restricted?
choices: Parking or driving on the same block is prohibited
Nothing — fire trucks handle their own zone
questions.explanation: You may not drive or park on the same block as a fire vehicle answering an alarm. Handbook p. 86.

questions.question: You're driving on a 2-lane road. An emergency vehicle with lights and siren comes up from the OPPOSITE direction. What should you do?
choices: Keep going — they're not in your lane
Speed up to clear past quickly
Pull to the right and slow or stop until they pass
questions.explanation: Even when emergency vehicles come from the opposite direction, pull to the right and slow/stop until they pass. Handbook p. 86.

questions.question: Who has the right-of-way at a roundabout?
questions.explanation: Yield to traffic already in the roundabout. Handbook p. 55.

questions.question: You approach a roundabout. There are no cars in it but a pedestrian is at the entry crosswalk. What do you do?
choices: Enter — pedestrians yield to cars at roundabouts
Yield to the pedestrian, then enter
Stop fully like a stop sign, then go
questions.explanation: Yield to pedestrians AND circulating traffic at a roundabout. Handbook p. 55.

questions.question: YELLOW pavement markings separate:
choices: Traffic moving in the same direction
questions.explanation: Yellow = opposing directions. Handbook p. 53. (DLD sample-test canonical fact.)

questions.question: WHITE pavement markings separate:
choices: Traffic moving in the same direction
questions.explanation: White = same direction. Handbook p. 53. (DLD sample-test canonical fact.)

questions.question: A broken (dashed) yellow line on YOUR side of a center line means:
questions.explanation: Dashed yellow on your side = passing permitted. Handbook p. 53.

questions.question: A solid yellow line on YOUR side of a center line means:
questions.explanation: Solid yellow on your side = no passing in your direction. Handbook p. 53.

questions.question: Double solid yellow lines mean:
choices: One side may pass, the other may not
questions.explanation: Double solid yellow = no passing either direction. Handbook p. 53.

questions.question: Double solid white lines (such as express-lane separators) mean:
choices: Same as a dashed white line
questions.explanation: Double solid white = crossing prohibited. Handbook p. 53.

questions.question: When may you legally cross a double solid white line into or out of an express lane?
questions.explanation: Crossing double white is prohibited except in a true emergency. Handbook p. 50.

questions.question: An 8-sided red sign means:
choices: Stop — come to a complete stop
questions.explanation: Octagonal red sign = STOP. Handbook p. 57.

questions.question: A triangular sign with a red border (point-down) means:
choices: Yield — slow down and give right-of-way to others
questions.explanation: Downward-pointing triangle = YIELD. Handbook p. 57.

questions.question: A square red sign with a horizontal white bar and 'DO NOT ENTER' means:
choices: You're approaching a one-way street going the wrong direction — do not enter
questions.explanation: Do Not Enter — you're going against the flow of a one-way road. Handbook p. 57.

questions.question: A red rectangular sign reading 'WRONG WAY' means:
choices: Stop and immediately turn around — you're driving against traffic
questions.explanation: Wrong Way = you're driving against traffic; stop and turn around safely. Handbook p. 57.

questions.question: A rectangular black-and-white sign with an arrow saying 'ONE WAY' means:
choices: Traffic flows only in the direction of the arrow
questions.explanation: One Way = traffic moves only in the arrow's direction. Handbook p. 57.

questions.question: A 'NO TURN ON RED' sign at an intersection means:
choices: You may turn right on red if traffic is clear
You may NOT turn right (or left, where posted) on a red light
Stop for a longer time before turning
questions.explanation: No Turn On Red overrides the default right-on-red allowance. Handbook p. 57.

questions.question: A black-and-white sign with a U-shaped arrow and a red slash means:
questions.explanation: U-turn prohibited at this location. Handbook p. 57.

questions.question: A sign with an arrow pointing right and 'KEEP RIGHT' means:
choices: Pass an obstruction on the right side
questions.explanation: Keep Right means pass the obstruction (median, sign, etc.) on the right. Handbook p. 57.

questions.question: A yellow pennant-shaped sign on the left side of the road means:
choices: No passing zone — passing prohibited in your direction
questions.explanation: Yellow pennant on the left = No Passing Zone marker. Handbook p. 57.

questions.question: A yellow diamond sign with a black curved arrow means:
choices: Curve in the road ahead — slow down
questions.explanation: Yellow diamond + curved arrow = curve ahead, slow down. Handbook p. 58.

questions.question: A yellow diamond sign showing two arrows joining together means:
choices: Merge — be ready for traffic merging into your lane
questions.explanation: Merge ahead — be ready for traffic joining your lane. Handbook p. 58.

questions.question: A yellow diamond sign showing two lanes narrowing to one means:
choices: Lane ends — merge into the remaining lane
questions.explanation: Lane ends — prepare to merge into the open lane. Handbook p. 58.

questions.question: A yellow diamond sign showing a red octagon with 'STOP AHEAD' means:
choices: A stop sign is coming — prepare to stop
questions.explanation: Stop sign ahead — prepare to stop. Handbook p. 58.

questions.question: A yellow diamond sign showing traffic-light symbols means:
choices: Signal ahead — prepare to stop if it turns red
questions.explanation: Signal Ahead — be ready for a traffic light. Handbook p. 58.

questions.question: A pentagon-shaped yellow-green sign with two children walking means:
choices: School zone or school crossing — slow down, watch for children
questions.explanation: Pentagon school sign — school zone or crossing, watch for kids. Handbook p. 58.

questions.question: A 5-sided (pentagon) sign always means:
questions.explanation: Pentagon = school. Handbook p. 58.

questions.question: Fluorescent yellow-green signs typically indicate:
questions.explanation: Fluorescent yellow-green = school/ped/bike warnings. Handbook p. 58.

questions.question: A yellow-green pentagon sign with a single pedestrian figure means:
choices: Pedestrian crossing — watch for people on foot
questions.explanation: Pedestrian crossing warning. Handbook p. 58.

questions.question: A yellow-green sign with a bicycle figure means:
questions.explanation: Bike crossing warning. Handbook p. 58.

questions.question: A yellow diamond sign with a deer silhouette means:
choices: Deer crossing — watch for animals on the road
questions.explanation: Animal crossing warning. Handbook p. 58.

questions.question: A yellow diamond sign with a car and curvy tire marks means:
questions.explanation: Slippery road conditions warning. Handbook p. 58.

questions.question: A yellow diamond sign showing a downhill grade and percentage (e.g. 7%) means:
choices: Steep hill ahead — gear down, control speed
questions.explanation: Steep grade ahead — control speed. Handbook p. 58.

questions.question: A yellow rectangular sign below a curve warning showing '25' means:
choices: The legal speed limit is 25 mph
The advisory (recommended safe) speed for the curve is 25 mph
questions.explanation: Advisory speed = recommended safe speed for the hazard. Yellow advisory is suggestion; black-on-white speed limit signs are law. Handbook p. 58.

questions.question: An X-shaped white sign reading 'RAILROAD CROSSING' indicates:
choices: A railroad crosses the road here — yield to trains
questions.explanation: Crossbuck marks an active railroad crossing. Handbook p. 57.

questions.question: An orange diamond sign with workers shown means:
choices: Work zone — slow down, expect changes
questions.explanation: Orange diamond = work zone warning. Handbook p. 58.

questions.question: An orange diamond sign with a person holding a paddle means:
choices: A flagger may be directing traffic ahead — be ready to obey
questions.explanation: Flagger ahead — be ready to follow their directions. Handbook p. 58.

questions.question: An orange sign with an arrow and 'DETOUR' means:
choices: Road is closed ahead; follow the alternate route shown
questions.explanation: Detour — follow the alternate route. Handbook p. 58.

questions.question: An octagon (8 sides) shape is reserved for which sign?
questions.explanation: Only stop signs are octagonal. Handbook p. 57.

questions.question: A downward-pointing triangle is reserved for which sign?
questions.explanation: Downward triangle = yield. Handbook p. 57.

questions.question: A yellow diamond shape typically means:
questions.explanation: Yellow diamond = warning. Handbook p. 58.

questions.question: Red signs generally mean:
questions.explanation: Red signs = stop, prohibition, or do-not-enter. Handbook p. 57.

questions.question: Yellow signs generally mean:
questions.explanation: Yellow = warning. Handbook p. 58.

questions.question: Orange signs typically mean:
questions.explanation: Orange = work zone. Handbook p. 58.

questions.question: Green signs typically indicate:
questions.explanation: Green = guide signs / permitted movements. Handbook p. 58.

questions.question: Blue signs typically indicate:
questions.explanation: Blue = services. Handbook p. 58.

questions.question: Brown signs typically indicate:
questions.explanation: Brown = recreation. Handbook p. 58.

questions.question: Which of these does NOT require a complete stop?
choices: Entering a street from a private driveway
questions.explanation: Complete stops are required at steady red, flashing red, all stop signs, after a crash, when police request, and entering a road from an alley/driveway/private road. Yellow = stop IF you can do so safely — not required to. Handbook p. 45.

questions.question: A flashing red light requires what kind of stop?
choices: A complete stop (treat like a stop sign)
No stop required if no traffic visible
questions.explanation: Flashing red = complete stop, treat as stop sign. Handbook p. 45, p. 56.

questions.question: How early should you signal before turning?
choices: At least 2 seconds before the turn
questions.explanation: Signal at least 2 seconds before turning. Handbook p. 44.

questions.question: How early should you signal before a lane change?
questions.explanation: Signal at least 2 seconds before any lane change. Handbook p. 44.

questions.question: You're driving straight through an intersection and realize your exit is one lane to the right. What's the correct move?
choices: Signal and change lanes while inside the intersection
Complete the straight path, exit the intersection, then signal and change lanes
Stop in the intersection and let cars pass
questions.explanation: Don't change lanes inside an intersection. Exit it first, then signal and change. Handbook p. 41.

questions.question: What's the correct right turn procedure?
choices: Wide swing to make the turn easier, signal as you turn
Signal 2 sec, head check right shoulder, traffic check, don't cut corner or make wide turn
Just signal — the rest happens naturally
questions.explanation: Right turn: signal 2 sec → head check right → traffic check → no cutting/no wide turn. Handbook p. 46.

questions.question: What's the correct left turn procedure?
choices: Wide swing across the lane, signal as you complete
Signal 2 sec, head check left shoulder, traffic check, don't cut corner or make wide turn
questions.explanation: Left turn: signal 2 sec → head check left → traffic check → no cutting/no wide turn. Handbook p. 47.

questions.question: After completing a turn, you should:
choices: Stay in whatever lane the turn ended in
Finish a left turn in the left-most lane (next to centerline) and a right turn in the right-most (curb) lane
Move to the center lane immediately
questions.explanation: Left turn finishes in left-most lane; right turn finishes in right-most. Handbook p. 41.

questions.question: When turning at a multi-lane intersection, you should:
choices: Pick whichever lane is open mid-turn
Enter and stay in the same lane until the turn is completed
Change lanes once you start the turn
Drift across two lanes during the turn
questions.explanation: On multi-lane turns, stay in the lane you entered until the turn is completed. Handbook p. 47.

questions.question: You may NOT make a U-turn where:
choices: You don't have at least 500 feet of visibility in both directions
questions.explanation: U-turn requires 500 ft sight in both directions (no curve, no hill blocking view). Handbook p. 48.

questions.question: U-turns are prohibited in all of these locations EXCEPT:
choices: On a 2-lane road with 500+ feet visibility both directions
questions.explanation: U-turns are banned where a sign prohibits, on RR crossings, on freeways. A 2-lane road with adequate sight is OK. Handbook p. 48.

questions.question: May you drive across or within a traffic island (e.g., painted or raised median pad)?
choices: No — never drive over, across, or within any part of an island
questions.explanation: Don't drive over or within any traffic island. Handbook p. 47.

questions.question: The left lane on a multi-lane road is intended for:
questions.explanation: Left lane is for passing. Handbook p. 44.

questions.question: You're cruising in the left lane at the posted speed. A faster car comes up behind clearly wanting to pass. What must you do?
choices: Stay — you're at the legal limit
Speed up to keep them behind
Move safely to the right — failing to do so can result in an impeding-traffic citation
questions.explanation: When approached from behind in the left lane, move right safely. Impeding traffic = citation. Handbook p. 49.

questions.question: By default, you should travel in which lane on a multi-lane road?
questions.explanation: Stay in the right-most lane unless blocked. Handbook p. 41.

questions.question: The emergency lane (outside the solid white line on a freeway) may be used for:
choices: Picking up or dropping off riders
Genuine emergencies only — no driving, passing, or pickup
questions.explanation: Emergency lane = emergencies only. Handbook p. 49.

questions.question: May you make a U-turn on a freeway?
choices: Yes, where there's an emergency-only opening in the median
Yes, between exits if traffic is light
Never — no U-turns on the freeway
questions.explanation: No U-turns on the freeway. Handbook p. 49.

questions.question: On a flex lane, what does a green ARROW overhead mean and what does a red X mean?
choices: Green arrow = lane is open in your direction; red X = lane is closed
Green arrow = stop; red X = go
questions.explanation: Flex lane: green arrow = open, red X = closed. Handbook p. 49.

questions.question: Utah's basic speed law says:
choices: Always drive at the posted speed limit
Never drive faster than is reasonably safe for current conditions
Always drive 5 mph under the limit
Drive whatever speed traffic is moving
questions.explanation: Basic speed law: never drive faster than is reasonably safe for the conditions. Handbook p. 54.

questions.question: What's the speed limit in an active school zone?
questions.explanation: School zone speed limit is 20 mph when lights are flashing, children present, or during opening/closing hours. Handbook p. 54.

questions.question: What's the default speed limit in a business or residential area (unless posted otherwise)?
questions.explanation: 25 mph in business or residential areas. Handbook p. 54.

questions.question: What's the default maximum speed limit on a major highway (as posted)?
questions.explanation: 55 mph on major highways as posted. Handbook p. 54.

questions.question: Speed limits on Utah's rural interstate highways may be posted as high as:
questions.explanation: Utah rural interstates may be posted up to 80 mph. Handbook p. 54.

questions.question: May you legally drive faster than the posted speed limit when matching traffic flow?
choices: Yes, if you're going with the flow
No — never legally exceed the posted speed limit
questions.explanation: Never legally drive faster than the posted limit, even when matching flow. Handbook p. 49.

questions.question: You're on I-15 in the right lane at the posted 70 mph. All traffic around you is going 80. What's your legal max?
choices: 70 mph — never exceed the posted limit
questions.explanation: Legal max is always the posted limit, regardless of traffic flow. Handbook p. 49.

questions.question: When does the 20 mph school-zone limit apply?
choices: When lights are flashing, children are present, or during school opening/closing hours
questions.explanation: 20 mph school zone applies when flashing lights are on, children are present, or during opening/closing hours. Handbook p. 54.

questions.question: You drive past a school at 8:00 a.m. The school-zone speed sign is flashing. You see no kids on the street. What's your max speed?
choices: Posted regular limit — no kids visible
20 mph — flashing lights mean the lower limit is active
questions.explanation: Flashing lights = school zone speed applies, kids visible or not. Handbook p. 91.

questions.question: You're driving through a neighborhood with no posted speed limit signs. What's your max?
choices: There's no default — drive what feels safe
questions.explanation: Default in residential = 25 mph. Handbook p. 54.

questions.question: What's the MINIMUM following distance on a freeway in good conditions?
questions.explanation: Minimum 2-second following distance on the freeway. Increase under bad conditions. Handbook p. 49.

questions.question: How do you measure a 2-second following distance?
choices: Count the car lengths between you and the car ahead
When the car ahead passes a fixed point, count 'one-thousand-one, one-thousand-two' before you reach the same point
questions.explanation: Watch the car ahead pass a fixed point (sign, line), then count until you reach the same point. If less than 2 seconds, you're too close. Handbook p. 49.

questions.question: How should you adjust your following distance at night?
choices: Add at least 2 seconds to your following distance
questions.explanation: Add at least 2 seconds at night (so 4+ seconds total). Handbook p. 49.

questions.question: You're on I-15 in heavy rain. The car ahead is hard to see clearly. What should you do with your following distance?
choices: Increase your following distance — 2 seconds is the minimum in good conditions, bad conditions need more
Decrease to 1 second so you can react faster
Following distance is irrelevant in rain
questions.explanation: 2 seconds is the MINIMUM in good conditions. Increase in rain, fog, snow, or darkness. Handbook p. 49.

questions.question: When following a motorcycle, what minimum following distance should you keep?
choices: Half the distance of a car
questions.explanation: Same 2-second minimum for motorcycles. Motorcycles can stop quickly — give them room. Handbook p. 49.

questions.question: When stopping behind another vehicle, what's a safe gap rule?
choices: Stop where your bumper touches theirs
Stop where you can see the vehicle's rear tires
Stop where you can read their license plate
questions.explanation: Safe gap when stopping behind another car: see their rear tires. Handbook p. 41, p. 46.

questions.question: You must use headlights when you cannot clearly see a person or vehicle at what distance?
questions.explanation: Use headlights when visibility is less than 1,000 ft. Handbook p. 49.

questions.question: Should you use headlights in rain or snow?
choices: No, they reflect off the precipitation
Yes — headlights help others see you in rain or snow
questions.explanation: Use headlights in rain or snow so other drivers can see you. Handbook p. 49.

questions.question: You must dim high beams within how many feet of an ONCOMING vehicle?
questions.explanation: Dim high beams within 500 ft of an oncoming vehicle. Handbook p. 49.

questions.question: You must dim high beams within how many feet when following another vehicle?
questions.explanation: Dim within 300 ft when following another vehicle. Handbook p. 49.

questions.question: Hydroplaning becomes a serious risk at what speed on wet roads?
questions.explanation: Hydroplaning risk rises sharply above ~35 mph on wet roads. Handbook p. 49.

questions.question: How much water on the road is enough to stall most cars?
questions.explanation: 6 inches of water can stall cars. Handbook p. 49.

questions.question: How much moving water can carry away most SUVs or trucks?
questions.explanation: 2 feet of moving water can carry away most SUVs and trucks. Handbook p. 49.

questions.question: You're driving and see water flowing across the road ahead — depth uncertain. What should you do?
choices: Drive through slowly to test it
questions.explanation: Turn around, don't drown. Never drive through standing or flowing water of uncertain depth. Handbook p. 49.

questions.question: A deer jumps onto the road in front of you. What do you do?
choices: Swerve hard to avoid hitting it
Brake firmly and stay in your lane — swerving causes worse crashes
Speed up to clear the area
questions.explanation: Don't swerve for animals — brake firmly and stay in your lane. Swerving causes worse crashes. Handbook p. 49.

questions.question: Wearing sunglasses at night is:
choices: Not recommended — they reduce your already-limited night vision
questions.explanation: Don't wear sunglasses at night — they reduce night visibility. Handbook p. 49.

questions.question: Total stopping distance includes which two components?
questions.explanation: Total stopping = perception/reaction distance + braking distance. Handbook p. 49.

questions.question: About how much total distance does a typical CAR need to stop at 55 mph?
questions.explanation: A car at 55 mph needs roughly 130–140 ft total. Handbook p. 49.

questions.question: About how much distance does a fully-loaded SEMI need to stop at 55 mph?
questions.explanation: A semi at 55 mph needs about 400 ft total — roughly 3x a passenger car. Handbook p. 49.

questions.question: You're driving 60 mph on the freeway. Roughly how many feet would 2 seconds of following distance represent?
questions.explanation: At 60 mph (~88 ft/s), 2 seconds ≈ 175 ft. Handbook p. 49.

questions.question: It's snowing heavily. What's the right following-distance practice?
choices: Drop to 1 second — visibility is limited so you need quicker reaction
Stick at 2 seconds — that's the legal minimum
Increase well beyond 2 seconds — bad conditions require more space
Tailgate so the car ahead clears the road for you
questions.explanation: Bad conditions = increase your following distance beyond the 2-second minimum. Handbook p. 49.

questions.question: Speeding in a work zone:
choices: Is excused if no workers are present
questions.explanation: Double fines in work zones. Handbook p. 49.

questions.question: You're driving through a work zone at 11 p.m. and no workers are visible. What's the speed rule?
choices: You can go the regular limit — no workers, no risk
Slow to the work zone limit anyway — speed rules apply whether workers are present or not
questions.explanation: Work zone speed limits apply at all times, workers present or not. Handbook p. 49.

questions.question: Roughly what share of fatalities in work zones are NOT workers?
choices: About 80% — most are drivers and passengers
questions.explanation: About 80% of work zone fatalities are drivers and passengers, not workers. Slowing protects YOU too. Handbook p. 49.

questions.question: Orange diamond signs indicate:
choices: Work zone — be ready for slowdowns, lane shifts, flaggers
questions.explanation: Orange = work zone. Handbook p. 58.

questions.question: You're approaching a steep blind hill at the posted 55 mph limit. What's the safe driving rule?
choices: The posted limit is always safe — go 55
Adjust speed down so you can stop within the distance you can see
questions.explanation: Basic speed law: never drive faster than is safe for conditions. Slow at blind crests. Handbook p. 54.

questions.question: A yellow advisory speed sign reads '30' under a curve warning. The posted limit on the road is 55. What speed should you drive?
choices: 30 mph or less for the curve — the advisory is the recommended safe speed for the curve
55 mph until you enter the curve
questions.explanation: Advisory speeds are the recommended safe speed for the hazard. Slow to that or below. Handbook p. 54.

questions.question: Heavy fog reduces visibility to about 100 feet. You're on a 65 mph road. What's the right speed?
choices: 65 mph — the posted limit is the law
Slow significantly — you must be able to stop within the distance you can see
Drive faster to clear the fog area
Same as the limit if you use fog lights
questions.explanation: Basic speed law: drive only as fast as safe for conditions. Heavy fog = slow well below the posted limit. Handbook p. 54.

questions.question: You're cutting through a neighborhood on the way to school. There are no posted speed signs and kids are playing in driveways. What's your max?
questions.explanation: Residential default = 25 mph. Slow further if kids are visible. Handbook p. 54.

questions.question: True or false: driving the posted speed limit is always legal, regardless of conditions.
choices: False — basic speed law requires you to drive at a speed that's reasonably safe; the posted limit is a maximum, not a guarantee
questions.explanation: Posted limit is a max — basic speed law requires you to drive only as fast as safe. Handbook p. 54.

questions.question: An orange triangle (slow-moving-vehicle emblem) on a vehicle ahead means the vehicle's max speed is:
questions.explanation: The slow-moving-vehicle triangle means the vehicle travels 25 mph or less. Handbook p. 58.

questions.question: Under good conditions on a freeway, the recommended following distance is at LEAST:
questions.explanation: Minimum 2-second following distance on the freeway in good conditions. Handbook p. 49.

questions.question: You're behind a large semi on the freeway. You can't see past it. What should you do?
choices: Tailgate so you can pass when you see a gap
Increase your following distance so you can see further ahead and so the truck driver can see you
Speed up to pass even without a clear lane
Drive in the truck's blind spot
questions.explanation: Increase following distance behind a large truck so you can see further ahead and the truck driver can see you in their mirrors. Handbook p. 49.

questions.question: Doubling your speed roughly multiplies the BRAKING distance by:
questions.explanation: Braking distance scales with the square of speed — double speed ≈ 4x braking distance. Handbook p. 49.

questions.question: Which is the highest speed at which it's always LEGAL to drive on Utah roads?
questions.explanation: Never exceed the posted limit. The posted number is the legal max. Handbook p. 49.
